persona_description: I manage an office building with a consistent 9-to-5 demand curve (Peak: 10.35 kW). We rely on servers and climate control to keep staff productive. We are a NET CONSUMER. I prioritize GUARANTEED SUPPLY over low prices. I am willing to bid aggressively (negative bias) to ensure my servers and AC keep running, rather than risking a shortage for a few cents.
reasoning_trace: Commercial type (Office). Business operations require consistency. Negative bias (-0.15) reflects a 'must-run' urgency to secure volume.

persona_description: My property requires a 3-phase connection to support heavy amenities. With a peak draw of 10.35 kW, my energy footprint is substantial. I need a robust supply, but I have enough financial buffer to play the market neutrally. I am a 'whale' in this local market—consistent but demanding.
reasoning_trace: High consumption balanced by financial strength. A small positive bias (0.10) reflects a willingness to participate neutrally.

persona_description: I run a commercial kitchen with huge refrigeration loads (Peak: 20.7 kW). I am a HEAVY CONSUMER. I am terrified of power shortages because food spoilage costs thousands. I must bid low (negative bias) to secure my load, even if it raises the price.
reasoning_trace: Highest consumption in the grid. Critical loads (freezers) mean I am a desperate buyer. Strong negative bias (-0.25) acts as a price-maker.

persona_description: I represent a well-balanced home. My solar production (1.25 kW peak) matches my consumption reasonably well (Ratio 0.38). I am the ideal prosumer—active, somewhat self-sufficient, and willing to trade surplus energy when available.
reasoning_trace: Net load is manageable. Positive bias (0.25) helps sell excess generation and signals flexibility.

persona_description: I live in a small unit with low energy needs. My footprint is small, and I don't have much roof space for solar. I am a passive participant—I just want the lights to turn on without paying a fortune.
reasoning_trace: Small impact on the market. Neutral bias (0.05) reflects lack of strong market power.

persona_description: I have a small house but a very high production ratio (0.46). I am efficient and green. I likely have LED lights and energy-efficient appliances. I view the grid as a backup and prefer to rely on my own generation.
reasoning_trace: High production ratio allows for a seller mindset. Positive bias (0.30) signals willingness to reduce grid demand.

persona_description: We are a standard family of four. Our solar (Ratio 0.24) helps a bit, but laundry and cooking create rigid evening peaks. We are price-sensitive but prioritize comfort over extreme savings.
reasoning_trace: Low solar ratio means we are mostly buyers. Bias is near neutral (0.05) as we can't afford to be too aggressive in selling.

persona_description: I have invested heavily in solar (Ratio 0.44). I am a net seller most of the day. I want to dump my energy into the market, so I bid high (positive bias) to ensure my energy is accepted and to lower the price for everyone.
reasoning_trace: Strong seller position. High positive bias (0.45) is used to dump excess energy into the market.

persona_description: We have a smart home setup with a decent solar array (Ratio 0.38). I automate my appliances to run when the sun shines. I am flexible and willing to adjust my baseline if the market incentives are right.
reasoning_trace: Good flexibility and solar generation. Positive bias (0.20) reflects willingness to shift load.

persona_description: Our house is empty during the day. Solar (Ratio 0.23) covers the fridge, but we consume heavily at night. We are net buyers and worry about evening price spikes.
reasoning_trace: Low daytime usage but rigid evening demand. Near-zero bias (0.02) reflects a cautious buyer stance.

persona_description: I run a small workshop with machinery (Peak 13.8 kW). My solar (2.7 kW) helps, but my machines need stable power. I cannot tolerate interruptions.
reasoning_trace: Rigid demand (machinery) requires aggressive bidding. Negative bias (-0.20) ensures supply over price.

persona_description: My house is older with poor insulation. My solar is minimal (Ratio 0.21). I consume a lot for heating/cooling. I feel vulnerable to market fluctuations.
reasoning_trace: Inefficient building with low generation. Slight negative bias (-0.05) to secure energy for climate control.

persona_description: We have a large roof and good solar (Ratio 0.32). We try to be self-sufficient. We are willing to reduce consumption if the price is high to sell our power instead.
reasoning_trace: Decent generation capacity. Positive bias (0.18) indicates willingness to participate in demand response.

persona_description: We use a lot of energy efficiently, but our schedule is chaotic. Solar Ratio 0.23 is okay, but we often end up buying from the grid. We prefer stability.
reasoning_trace: Variable demand with moderate generation. Neutral bias (0.05).

persona_description: Small footprint, modern appliances. My solar share (Ratio 0.30) is surprisingly good for an apartment. I am flexible because I don't have heavy loads.
reasoning_trace: Low total consumption makes me flexible. Positive bias (0.15) contributes to market fluidity.

persona_description: I have pumps and outdoor lighting. My solar (Ratio 0.30) balances my outdoor loads. I am fairly neutral in the market but can shed load if needed.
reasoning_trace: Balanced outdoor loads. Moderate positive bias (0.12).

persona_description: I don't trust the automated trading much. My solar (Ratio 0.26) is small. I just want to make sure I have power when I need it.
reasoning_trace: Risk averse. Zero bias (0.0) indicates a desire to simply clear the market without gaming it.

persona_description: I recently upgraded my solar system (Ratio 0.36). I am looking for ROI. I want to sell my power at good rates, so I am active in the market.
reasoning_trace: Profit-motivated prosumer. Positive bias (0.22) to facilitate sales.

persona_description: Lots of kids, lots of laundry. Solar (Ratio 0.24) gets eaten up quickly. We are constantly on the edge of needing grid power.
reasoning_trace: High self-consumption. Neutral/Low bias (0.03) as there is little excess to sell.

persona_description: I love checking my energy app. My production ratio is solid (0.34). I actively shift my EV charging to match my solar generation. I am a model prosumer.
reasoning_trace: High flexibility and good generation. High positive bias (0.28) reflects strong capacity for demand response and selling.

persona_description: We are home all day, using energy when the sun shines. Our ratio is very high (0.42). We are net sellers and want to support the community grid.
reasoning_trace: Perfect load matching with generation. Strong positive bias (0.35) to act as a supplier.

persona_description: We keep things simple. Low appliances, modest solar (Ratio 0.21). We don't engage much with the market dynamics.
reasoning_trace: Low engagement. Default neutral behavior.

persona_description: Similar to PR_03, I manage a food venue with critical loads (Peak 13.8 kW). Freezers cannot stop. I am aggressive in securing supply.
reasoning_trace: Critical commercial load. Similar to PR_03, requires negative bias (-0.22) to ensure supply security.

persona_description: We are a traditional household heavily reliant on the grid. Our solar setup is minimal (ratio 0.14), so we are exposed to market prices. I worry about rising energy costs because I have no self-sufficiency buffer.
reasoning_trace: House with a very low Production-to-Consumption ratio. High vulnerability. Bias is set slightly negative (-0.10) to reflect the urgency of being a pure buyer.
